_comment: Source of truth for changelog.html's 'Recent updates' list. Regenerated by tools/gen_changelog.mjs from merged PRs carrying both the 'changelog:major' label and a '## What this means for you' section (see CONTRIBUTING.md). Entries are newest first. An entry may carry a validated optional 'media' object; reproduce assets with tools/capture_changelog_media.py. An entry may also carry an optional 'merged_prs' array (at least two {pr, url} pairs, including its own primary 'pr') when several related PRs were consolidated into one user story — see AGENTS.md's changelog section. Do not hand-edit the 'text' field without also updating changelog.html's auto-generated block (run: node tools/gen_changelog.mjs --rebuild).

entries.text: Some agencies don't file their awards in the City Record — they post them elsewhere. CROL-List now checks those outside sources for you: it shows what it finds, links to the source, and says so honestly when nothing has turned up yet. If a notice has no award on file, you can ask to be emailed the moment one appears — click "Email me when the award registers," confirm your email once, and you're done. No extra emails until there's real news.

recording.caption: Find a covered notice, choose the award watch, enter reader@example.com, and reach the email confirmation step.

entries.text: Click a link in a CROL-List digest email. You used to land on a bare notice page. Now you see why it matched. The matching word is highlighted. You also see a plain summary of your watch.

before.alt: A notice page opened from a bare link, with no sign of why it matched.

after.alt: The same notice page opened from a digest link, with the matching word highlighted and a plain summary of the watch.

entries.text: Has a saved alert gone quiet? If it finds nothing new for a couple of months, its next email says so. That email links back to the alerts page. Your exact search is already loaded there. You can broaden it in one step.

Notice pages now show whether a recurring bid was awarded before, and to whom — not just its own paper trail. When there's no confirmed match, you can choose "Show possible earlier rounds" to see looser, clearly-marked possibilities instead of a dead end.

The stats page's "all time" numbers, topic/lens breakdowns, and day-by-day history now say exactly how far back they can honestly account for — no more numbers that quietly undercount, and no more "since launch" where that isn't true. Digests, searches, and now active watches are also easier to read at a glance, with the technical plumbing (feed fetches, API checks) moved to its own clearly-labeled section at the bottom.

before.alt: The stats page with one flat grid mixing plain outcomes and technical plumbing, and no note on how far back the totals count.

after.alt: The stats page with headline numbers separated from a technical-details section, and a note on how far back each total counts.

entries.text: Typing a full sentence like "education contracts over $200K due in 3 months" into an alert now matches the category, amount, and deadline together — wherever you type it: the 60-second quiz, the "Ask" box, or Build an alert. You'll see the real, interpreted results before you subscribe. A single word or a quoted phrase still searches literally as before.

recording.caption: Pick the "City contracts and RFPs" topic, type a full sentence, and preview the real, interpreted results.

entries.text: Alert emails and the "Preview my digest" tool now show exactly which word matched and where — no more guessing why an unrelated-looking notice showed up in your search results.

Opening the Land or Staffing tab for the first time now shows a real, current example — a live rezoning application, or the city's most common job title with its salary band and career ladder — instead of an empty panel waiting for you to search. Your own searches and shared links work exactly as before.
The stats page now shows day-by-day history of digests sent and searches asked, with recovered and live-counted days honestly labeled.
You can now build an alert that watches one agency, or only awards, or only open solicitations, not just an amount or a deadline.
The stats page now uses plain-language labels, shows all-time totals, and breaks down digest matches by topic.
The Money tab is now called Contracts, and a notice page now leads with that notice's own facts before agency-wide totals.
The homepage now says how many notices came in today, digest email titles are clickable, and a new tagline explains what the site does.
You can now read every page in Arabic and Urdu, right to left.
You can now read the site in Bengali, Haitian Creole, Korean, French, and Polish.
You can now read the site in Chinese (Simplified) and Russian.
Closed the last known accessibility gaps: every control now has a visible focus ring, an accurate label, and enough color contrast to read.
Every page, not just the homepage, now translates into Español, and your language choice is remembered across visits.
